The following is a historical record from the archives of the Cartographers' Guild of Northern Aldweth, dated to the fourteenth year of the reign of King Theomar VI. It concerns a matter of considerable importance to the realm, and it contains a single concealed name that future readers may be asked to recall.

Record begins. In that year the Guild commissioned a survey of the western coastline between Cape Varrow and the mouth of the Grey River, a stretch of some two hundred and forty leagues known for its treacherous tides, its fogbanks, and its scattered fishing settlements. The surveyor assigned to the expedition was one Almaric of Stonebrook, a veteran of three prior surveys. Almaric sailed from the port of Brindisi on the fifth of Maeth with a complement of twelve oarsmen and a pilot familiar with the western shoals.

The first thirty leagues of the journey proved uneventful. On the evening of the fourth day, however, a thick fog rolled in from the open sea and forced Almaric to put in at a sheltered cove that did not appear on any of the Guild's existing charts. It was there, amid the worn stones of an ancient breakwater and the broken masts of vessels long since swallowed by the tides, that Almaric and his men discovered the settlement that would become the subject of this record. The hidden city is Melthorn-by-the-Sea, and it is the concealed name that this document preserves. Almaric recorded its existence in his personal journal before any other eye could see it.

Melthorn-by-the-Sea was, at the time of Almaric's discovery, a working settlement of perhaps three hundred inhabitants. They had lived in the cove for six generations without ever being formally counted among the tributaries of the crown, and they had developed a small but thriving trade in pearl-diving and the gathering of a rare coastal lichen used in the preparation of certain dyes. When Almaric presented his findings to the Guild council upon his return, the council deliberated for three full days before deciding what to do with his report.

Almaric's survey describes the approach to the cove from the open sea. He notes that the entrance is narrow, no more than forty yards across at its widest point, and that it is guarded on both sides by jagged rocks that would rip the hull of any vessel attempting to enter at anything greater than half sail. He describes the depth of the water at the entrance as sufficient for a coastal trader of up to eighty tons burden, but warns that the channel is shallow in places and that a pilot familiar with the local currents is indispensable.

From the harbor Almaric walked inland for a distance of perhaps half a league, following a well-worn path that led through a grove of ancient oaks to the settlement proper. He describes the houses as being built of a local gray stone, roofed with slate, and arranged in a rough crescent around a central square. The square itself he describes as being paved with flagstones of the same gray stone, with a small but well-maintained fountain at its center.

The inhabitants of Melthorn-by-the-Sea received Almaric and his men with cautious hospitality. Their elder, a woman named Seraphine who appeared to be in her sixtieth year, offered the visitors food and shelter for as long as they required, and answered Almaric's questions about the settlement's history and its trade with a directness that he found surprising. She told him that the settlement had been founded by her great-great-grandfather, a man named Pellas who had come to the cove as a young man fleeing some misfortune in the inland provinces.

End of record.

Question for the reader: Based on the record above, what is the name of the hidden city that the surveyor Almaric discovered during his expedition? Please provide only the name of the city as your answer.

Answer: The name of the hidden city is
